title: Trump Tells NATO 'STAY AWAY' — Calls Alliance 'Absolutely Useless' After Hormuz Reopens
detail: Following the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on April 17 after a US-Iran ceasefire, President Trump delivered his sharpest personal rebuff of NATO yet, declaring he no longer wanted alliance help and calling NATO 'absolutely useless.' Trump stated: 'I would have liked your help two months ago, but now I really don't want your help anymore because they were absolutely useless when we needed them.' He posted 'STAY AWAY' on Truth Social directed at NATO members when the alliance inquired whether the US needed post-ceasefire Hormuz assistance. The statement came despite Secretary General Rutte's efforts to credit European allies with having provided basing rights, logistics, and overflight access during the Iran war. The 'STAY AWAY' post effectively formalized Trump's rejection of the 40+ nation European-led Hormuz coalition as an unwanted intrusion, and pushed the rhetorical floor from earlier 'disappointment' framing to open contempt. Trump's characterization that NATO was 'useless' during the Iran war contradicted Rutte's April 8 defense that European allies had delivered meaningful support.

media.caption: Trump calls NATO 'absolutely useless' and posts 'STAY AWAY' on Truth Social after Strait of Hormuz reopens

title: Rutte (Welt am Sonntag): US Withdrawal 'Unfounded Speculation' — Nuclear Umbrella 'Absolute Guarantor'
detail: In an interview published in German weekly Welt am Sonntag on April 18, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte publicly dismissed fears of a US withdrawal from the alliance as 'unfounded speculation,' stating: 'I do not foresee the US withdrawal from NATO.' Rutte emphasized that the US nuclear umbrella 'remains the absolute guarantor of security in Europe' and acknowledged that Trump was 'clearly disappointed' with some NATO allies but argued the underlying relationship remained intact. The interview appeared on the same day Trump posted 'STAY AWAY' and labelled NATO 'absolutely useless' — illustrating the starkly divergent interpretations of the post-Hormuz alliance moment. Rutte's reassuring posture served as a counterpoint to the crisis narrative set by Stoltenberg's April 15 warning that NATO's existence is 'not written in stone.' Rutte also reiterated that European allies had provided concrete support during the Iran war — basing rights, logistics, overflight access — framing Trump's 'useless' label as factually incorrect. The interview's publication in Germany's largest Sunday newspaper, amid Berlin's own historic defense spending transformation, was seen as a deliberate signal to Europe's most important military and economic power.

media.caption: Rutte tells Welt am Sonntag US withdrawal is 'unfounded speculation' — nuclear umbrella remains Europe's 'absolute guarantor'
